title: Khamenei Laid to Rest as Iran's New Generation Flexes Power, Trump Pushes Peace on Two Fronts, Le Pen Awaits Verdict
subhead: Tehran's week-long funeral for Ali Khamenei draws millions — but his son and successor Mojtaba was conspicuously absent, wounded or in hiding. Meanwhile, Trump pressed both Putin and Zelenskyy toward a deal ahead of the NATO summit in Ankara, and France braces for a verdict that could reshape its next presidential election.

sections.heading: Khamenei's Funeral: A Nation Mourns, a New Generation Governs
sections.body:
Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's funeral proceedings began on Friday in Tehran, with Iranian authorities expecting 12 to 20 million people to attend ceremonies across Iran and Iraq over the coming week. Sunday's service at the Grand Mosalla religious complex drew President Masoud Pezeshkian, Revolutionary Guards chief Ahmad Vahidi, and three of Khamenei's sons. But the most notable absence was Mojtaba Khamenei — the new supreme leader, appointed in early March — who has not been seen in public since the US-Israel air strikes that killed his father in February. Rumours persist that he was wounded in the same attacks.

Trump, according to Axios, said peace talks had been paused for a week for the funeral proceedings, adding that Washington could take out Iran's leadership 'with one shot' but would not 'because then we would have nobody to negotiate with.' Mourners in Tehran held banners reading 'kill Trump' and 'kill Bibi,' while poet Mohammad Rasouli declared from the podium that 'Trump's murder is our responsibility,' drawing chants of 'death to America' and 'death to Israel.'

More than 4,000 people visited medical centres near the Grand Mosalla on Sunday, though no deaths were reported. Khamenei's coffin was displayed alongside those of four relatives killed in the strikes, including his one-year-old granddaughter. The burial is set for Thursday in Mashhad.
sections.whyItMatters: The funeral is both a genuine outpouring of grief and a carefully choreographed political moment for Iran's new leadership. Mojtaba Khamenei's absence raises questions about his health and authority at a time when the regime is attempting to project strength and continuity. The anti-American rhetoric on display complicates the fragile US-Iran diplomatic process.

sections.heading: Iran's Post-War Pragmatists: A New Regime With a New Calculus
sections.body:
As BBC diplomatic correspondent Paul Adams reports, the war between the US-Israel coalition and Iran has produced something few anticipated: a younger, more pragmatic generation of Iranian leaders who are both more aggressive in war and more willing to negotiate in peace. Vali Nasr of Johns Hopkins told the BBC that 'a whole new generation has taken over' with 'a very clear agenda,' adding that they 'managed the war and now they're going to manage the peace as well.' Sanam Vakil of Chatham House noted that the 'big handbrake on evolution of the system was Ali Khamenei' — now removed.

The new leadership — Mojtaba Khamenei (56), parliamentary speaker and chief negotiator Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, and IRGC commander Ahmad Vahidi — are all in their 60s or younger, all closely tied to the Revolutionary Guard. Unlike the elder Khamenei's decades-long 'no war, no peace' doctrine, these leaders demonstrated a willingness to attack US bases across the region and close the Strait of Hormuz, taking the war to a standstill against the combined US-Israel military.

Ali Vaez of the International Crisis Group described the current moment as a 'plastic moment' — pregnant with possibility as old adversaries contemplate new relationships. Gulf states, caught in the crossfire, are reportedly putting out feelers to Tehran. AFP reported that Saudi Arabia may be preparing a 'reconciliation summit' with Iran and its Gulf neighbours. But Vaez cautioned that none are ready to sever ties with the US military, adding 'they don't have anywhere better to go.'

Since the Versailles MoU was signed last month, Iran has already benefited from American sanctions waivers allowing crude oil and petroleum exports for 60 days. The MoU also references a $300bn 'reconstruction and development' plan, though who funds it remains unclear. Vice President JD Vance has spoken of 'a fundamentally transformed relationship,' but analysts warn the scope for failure remains vast: disagreements over Iran's nuclear programme, the Strait of Hormuz, the war in Lebanon, and entrenched hardliners on all sides.
sections.whyItMatters: Iran's new leadership represents a genuine break from the cautious old guard — they are more willing to use force, more willing to negotiate, and more dependent on the IRGC. The question is whether their pragmatism extends to the domestic reforms needed to rebuild legitimacy after January's bloody crackdown on protests, or whether the war-forged nationalist momentum will simply entrench a new elite.

sections.heading: Trump Pushes Ukraine Peace Ahead of NATO Summit in Ankara
sections.body:
Trump spoke separately with both Putin and Zelenskyy on Saturday in nearly 90-minute calls, ahead of this week's NATO summit in Ankara on July 7-8. According to Kremlin aide Yury Ushakov, Trump offered to help Putin find a solution to the Ukraine war, describing the conversation as 'businesslike and quite constructive.' Ushakov said Putin depicted the situation on the battlefield where Russian forces were 'confidently advancing,' while accusing Kyiv and its European allies of seeking to extend and escalate the conflict.

Zelenskyy, for his part, said he and Trump discussed the war's 1,200km front line and that 'there is a real prospect to end this war and American resolve will have a crucial meaning.' A senior US official confirmed Trump would meet Zelenskyy on Wednesday, saying 'the battlefield has clearly frozen over the last couple of months and neither side is making a lot of progress' and that 'the president feels a real sense of urgency to try to bring this to a stop.'

The diplomatic push comes as both sides are effectively stalemated. According to the Institute for the Study of War, Russian territorial gains have collapsed from 2,190 sq km in the first half of 2025 to just 622 sq km this year — and a net gain of only 97 sq km when Ukrainian counter-advances are included. Ukraine's military estimates Russia suffered 39,490 casualties in June alone, far exceeding its estimated monthly recruitment capacity of 24,000-30,000. Ukraine has also escalated its long-range strike campaign, hitting oil refineries near St Petersburg and military facilities in Crimea.

The dispute over Kostiantynivka illustrates the fog of war: Russia claims to have captured the Donetsk city, which Ukraine denies. Zelenskyy challenged Putin to meet him there if the city were truly under Russian control. The Kremlin dismissed the idea, restating its invitation for Zelenskyy to come to Moscow.
sections.whyItMatters: The NATO summit in Ankara is the next critical juncture for the Ukraine war. With the front lines effectively frozen and Russian casualties unsustainable at current rates, both Putin and Zelenskyy are positioning for negotiations — but with very different expectations. Trump's willingness to press both sides simultaneously is the variable that could shift the equation, though US diplomatic bandwidth remains divided with the Iran file still active.

sections.heading: France Awaits Le Pen Verdict: A Presidential Election in the Balance
sections.body:
Marine Le Pen will learn on Tuesday whether she can run for the French presidency in 2027, when a Paris appeal court rules on her embezzlement conviction. The 57-year-old National Rally leader, who leads in the polls, was found guilty in March 2025 of embezzling €1.4m in European Parliament funds to pay party employees as fake parliamentary assistants between 2004 and 2016. Her original five-year ban on holding public office, if upheld, would rule her out of the April 2027 election.

The court could reduce the ban to two years or less, which would free her to run. It could also impose a prison term with an electronic tag, which Le Pen has said would make it 'not possible' to campaign, telling LCI that 'when you are a presidential candidate you must be completely free to move about.' If barred, her 30-year-old lieutenant Jordan Bardella — currently polling marginally ahead of her at above 30% for the first round — would stand in her place.

The verdict fires the starting pistol on the presidential race regardless. The first round is set for 18 April 2027, with a run-off on 2 May. Opponents believe Le Pen would be a bigger threat than Bardella in a second round, given his relative inexperience. Conservative candidate Bruno Retailleau quipped that after a 40-year-old president, the French would 'certainly adore having a president of 30.'
sections.whyItMatters: France's next presidential election will be shaped by Tuesday's court decision. A Le Pen candidacy would test whether her 'detoxified' far-right brand can finally win the presidency; a Bardella candidacy raises different questions about experience and appeal. Either outcome reshapes European politics at a moment when the continent is rearming, dealing with the fallout of the Iran war, and facing a surge in right-wing populism across Latin America and Europe.

sections.heading: Super Typhoon Bavi Pummels US Pacific Territories
sections.body:
Super Typhoon Bavi made landfall on Guam and the Northern Mariana Islands on Sunday, packing winds of nearly 290km/h (180mph) with gusts up to 350km/h. The US National Weather Service warned of 'catastrophic' damage potential, with waves up to 11 metres. The island of Rota, population roughly 1,500, took a direct hit. A spokesperson for Rota's mayor told AFP that 'major damages' had already been reported, with the NWS warning that most of the area would be 'uninhabitable for weeks, perhaps longer.'

On Saipan, wind gusts of over 161km/h were recorded at the airport. Many residents were still without power from April's Super Typhoon Sinlaku, which killed 17 people and caused approximately $1.5bn in damage. Bavi is the 11th category four or five tropical cyclone to hit US territory in the past decade — one more than the total recorded in the prior 57 years. Scientists say warmer sea surface temperatures are driving more moisture into the atmosphere, supercharging storms.

Guam opened five evacuation centres, with one already at capacity by Sunday afternoon. Businesses boarded up, flights were cancelled, and residents hunkered down. Winds are not forecast to fall below typhoon force until early afternoon Monday.
sections.whyItMatters: Bavi is the second super typhoon to hit these US territories in three months. The accelerating frequency of extreme-category storms in the Pacific — 11 in the past decade versus 10 in the previous 57 years — is a direct climate signal. For the US military, which bases significant assets on Guam, the pattern raises hard questions about the resilience of Pacific infrastructure.

sections.heading: China-Russia Naval Drills Signal Alignment Amid NATO Summit
sections.body:
China and Russia begin annual joint naval exercises off Qingdao on Monday, running through July 13, followed by joint maritime patrols in the Pacific Ocean. Russia's Pacific Fleet has sent a cruiser, corvette, diesel-electric submarine, and rescue vessel. China's Northern Theatre Command is contributing two destroyers, a frigate, a submarine, a supply ship, and a rescue vessel. The drills will cover reconnaissance, air and missile defence, and surface-strike operations.

The exercises come roughly two months after Putin's trip to China, where he described bilateral relations as having reached an 'unprecedentedly high level,' and Xi Jinping called the partnership 'unyielding.' China has never denounced Russia's invasion of Ukraine and regularly calls for peace talks while insisting it is a neutral party. The timing, ahead of the NATO summit in Ankara, is a pointed reminder of the alternative alignment that both powers want to project.
sections.whyItMatters: The Qingdao drills are routine in timing but loaded in context. With NATO leaders gathering in Ankara to discuss Ukraine and European security, Beijing and Moscow are broadcasting that their partnership remains intact — and operationally capable — regardless of Western pressure. The inclusion of submarine and air defence exercises suggests the drills are meant to signal more than symbolism.

bottomLine: A week of funerals, summits, and verdicts. Iran laid Ali Khamenei to rest while his unseen successor Mojtaba's absence raised more questions than his presence would have answered. The new IRGC-linked generation running Tehran is more willing to fight and more willing to deal than the old guard — but the 'plastic moment' for a real regional reset depends on whether sanctions relief and reconstruction funding actually materialize. In Ukraine, Trump's twin calls to Putin and Zelenskyy ahead of the Ankara NATO summit signal a push for momentum, but the battlefield stalemate and Russia's unsustainable casualty rates mean neither side is negotiating from a position of obvious strength. France holds its breath for Tuesday's Le Pen verdict, which will determine whether the far-right's strongest candidate can stand in 2027. And Super Typhoon Bavi's devastation of US Pacific territories — the second such storm in three months — underscores the accelerating climate toll on military and civilian infrastructure alike.
